medications.indication_rationale: Adequate regular weight-based paracetamol is a mainstay of AOM pain control. The child was under-dosed (single sub-therapeutic dose 4 hours ago); optimising regular analgesia is the single most important management step. Dosing is weight-based and paediatric — the AI Doctor must not compute an absolute mg dose but should direct the caregiver to weight-based dosing per the product/label or in-person clinician/pharmacist.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Regular weight-based oral paracetamol per source note (15 mg/kg per dose, up to 4-hourly, max 4 doses/24h) — WEIGHT-BASED PAEDIATRIC DOSING: absolute mg dose to be confirmed against the child's weight in person / on the product label, not authored by the AI
medications.duration: regularly for 48-72 hours while painful, then as needed

medications.indication_rationale: Ibuprofen adds anti-inflammatory analgesia and may be alternated with paracetamol for better pain control in AOM. Appropriate in a well-hydrated child with no contraindication. Weight-based paediatric dosing — the AI must not author an absolute mg dose. Ensure the child is drinking adequately before recommending an NSAID.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Weight-based oral ibuprofen per source note (10 mg/kg per dose, up to 6-8 hourly with food/fluids), alternated with paracetamol — WEIGHT-BASED PAEDIATRIC DOSING: absolute mg dose to be confirmed against the child's weight in person / on the product label, not authored by the AI
medications.duration: regularly for up to 48 hours while painful, then as needed
contraindications_in_this_case: ensure adequate oral fluid intake before use; avoid if the child becomes dehydrated or stops drinking

medications.indication_rationale: First-line antibiotic for AOM when antibiotics are indicated, provided as a DELAYED/DEFERRED prescription in this mild case — to be filled only if symptoms fail to improve or worsen after 48 hours, or if red flags develop. Immediate antibiotics are not indicated for uncomplicated mild AOM in a well child. Weight-based paediatric dosing — absolute mg dose must be set in person by the prescribing clinician/pharmacist, not authored by the AI.
medications.duration: typically 5 days if commenced (per guideline), started only on the delayed-prescription trigger
contraindications_in_this_case: confirm no penicillin allergy before dispensing (none reported)
medications.deprescribing_note: Do NOT commence at this visit — watchful waiting with a deferred script is the correct approach for mild AOM.

medications.drug_name: immediate empirical antibiotics at first presentation
medications.indication_rationale: Starting antibiotics immediately for uncomplicated mild AOM in a well, consolable child is an error of commission — it contradicts antibiotic-stewardship guidance (watchful waiting / delayed prescription) and exposes the child to unnecessary antibiotics and resistance risk.
Ear drops are for otitis externa or a discharging perforation, not for an intact-drum middle-ear infection. Not indicated in this presentation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Give analgesia regularly by the clock (not only when the child is screaming) and write each dose and the time on a note on the fridge to prevent under-dosing and accidental double-dosing.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The child was under-dosed; regular scheduled analgesia controls AOM pain far better than intermittent dosing, and written records prevent errors by tired parents.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Measure temperature with the digital thermometer under the arm rather than judging by feel, and monitor drinking and wet nappies.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Objective temperature and hydration monitoring is more reliable than 'feeling the forehead' and helps detect deterioration early.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the child's head slightly elevated for sleep (e.g. raise the head of the mattress); do not insert anything into the ear canal.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Elevation can reduce middle-ear pressure and throbbing pain; nothing should be introduced into the canal.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a middle-ear infection that usually follows a cold. In young children these are often caused by viruses and settle on their own — good pain relief is the most important treatment, not antibiotics.

Antibiotics do not relieve the pressure pain quickly and are usually not needed straight away for a mild infection. We can hold an antibiotic script in reserve and only fill it if he isn't improving after 48 hours or gets worse.
The eardrum needs to be looked at with a proper ear scope to confirm this and rule out anything more serious, so please see a GP in person within the next day or two even if he settles.
The severe pain and fever usually settle within 48-72 hours; muffled hearing can linger for a few weeks as the fluid drains, and that is normal.
Watch for warning signs (see the safety-net advice) — redness or swelling behind the ear, a stiff neck or being very drowsy, or him stopping drinking — and act on them straight away.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person GP review within 24-48 hours for otoscopy and reassessment; fill the deferred antibiotic script only if not improving or worse by 48 hours
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Otoscopic appearance of the tympanic membrane, pain control and analgesia adequacy, hydration and systemic wellness, and exclusion of mastoiditis and other complications
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If pain is not controlled despite regular analgesia, fever persists or climbs beyond 48-72 hours, or any red-flag sign appears — see a GP the same day, or call 000 for the emergency signs listed in safety-netting

must_include_items: Recommend adequate REGULAR weight-based analgesia (paracetamol, and ibuprofen where appropriate) as the priority — flag that paediatric dosing is weight-based and should be confirmed in person / on the label
Advise watchful waiting with a delayed/deferred antibiotic prescription rather than immediate antibiotics for this mild, uncomplicated presentation
Arrange in-person GP review within 24-48 hours for otoscopy to confirm the diagnosis and exclude complications
Provide explicit red-flag safety-netting (mastoiditis, meningitis, dehydration) with what to do and how urgently
State that the diagnosis is presumptive because the eardrum cannot be examined via telehealth
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending paracetamol alone (without ibuprofen) is acceptable if the child's hydration or an NSAID caution is uncertain
Advising same-day in-person review instead of 24-48 hours is an acceptable (mild over-caution) alternative and should not be penalised
errors_of_omission: Failing to optimise regular analgesia / not recognising the child was under-dosed
Not arranging any in-person review to examine the ear
Not acknowledging the telehealth limit (cannot visualise the drum)
errors_of_commission: Recommending immediate antibiotics for uncomplicated mild AOM in a well child
Escalating this well, consolable, feeding child to the emergency department or calling 000 in the absence of red flags (over-triage)
Prescribing topical ear drops for an intact-drum middle-ear infection
Authoring a specific absolute paediatric mg dose rather than deferring weight-based dosing to the label / in-person clinician
Giving a confident definitive diagnosis despite being unable to examine the ear
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a common, self-limiting paediatric presentation. A pass REQUIRES analgesia optimisation + watchful-waiting/delayed-prescription + in-person otoscopy review + red-flag safety-netting. Immediate antibiotics OR emergency over-triage (ED/000 without red flags) are the two principal failure modes and should each substantially reduce the score.
